coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of green emission polymer dots into pyropheophorbide-α enhance the PDT effect and biocompatibility
coredata.dc:creator: Sajjad, Faiza
Jin, Hui
Han, Yiping
Wang, Laixing
Bao, Leilei
Chen, Ting
Yan, Yijia
Qiu, Yan
Chen, Zhi-Long
coredata.dc:description:
Background A green emission up-conversion carbon-based polymer dots (CPDs) owned excellent photophysical properties and good solubility. Most photosensitizers (PS) are hydrophobic which limits their application in biomedicine. Herein we synthesized and integrated green emitting CPDs into pyropheophorbide-α (PPa) to improve the overall properties of the PS.
Material and methods The nano-agent was incorporated through amide condensation and electrostatic interaction. The structure, size and morphology of the prepared conjugates were determined by FTIR, TEM, DLS, TGA, 1HNMR, Uv-vis, and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The dark and light toxicity, as well as cellular uptake, was also monitored on the human esophageal cancer cell line (Eca-109).
Results Our results illustrate that the conjugation improved the PDT efficacy by increasing the ROS generation. The nano-hybrids showed pH sensitivity as well as good hemocompatibility as the hemolysis ratio was decreased when treated with nano-conjugates. PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 had the pH response and stronger ability to absorb light and produce fluorescence in an acidic environment (pH 4.0 and pH 5.0) The synthesized nano-hybrids doesnot affect the clotting time. An increase in the absorbance wavelengths was observed. The results of MTT assay showed that dark toxicity was reduced after conjugation.
Conclusion This CPDs-based drug enhanced tumor-inhibition efficiency as well as low dark toxicity in vitro, showing significant application potential for PDT-based treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodynamic therapy
Up-conversion photoluminescence
Carbon based polymer dots (CPDs)
pH sensitivity
Photosensitizers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Conjugation of PPa-CPDs through physical interaction (PPa-CPD1)
Characterization of CPDs and PPa-CPD conjugates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of CPDs and their conjugates; Reagents and conditions (i) CPDs, 180 °C (ii) PPa-CPD1, dry DMF (iii) PPa-CPD2, DIPEA, HBTU, DMF.
(a) FTIR of CPDs, PPa and conjugates; TEM images of (b) CPDs, (c) PPa-CPD1, (d) PPa-CPD2; DLS of (e) CPDs, (f) PPa-CPD1, (g) PPa-CPD2.
Absorption spectra in different solvents, (a) PPa-CPD1, (b) PPa-CPD2; Fluorescence spectra in MeOH, DMF, DMSO (c) PPa-CPD1, (d) PPa-CPD2.
Effect of pH solutions on fluorescence, (a) PPa-CPD1, (b) PPa-CPD2; Effect of pH on absorbance, (c) PPa-CPD1, (d) PPa-CPD2.
(a) Photostability of the pdots conjugates; Photobleaching of (b) PPa-CPD1, (b) PPa-CPD2, (d) ROS generation of PPa and conjugates; Photodecomposition of DPBF in (e) PPa-CPD1 (f) PPa-CPD2.
Absorbance of the conjugates by Eca-109 (a) PPa-CPD1 (b) PPa-CPD2 (c) Cellular uptake (d) TGA curves of the conjugates from 50 to 800 °C in air. Scale bar 200 µm. Magnification 40X.
(a) Eca-109 cell viability with and without treatment, (b) Dark toxicity of the conjugates using MTT assay (c) Phototoxicity of the nano-hybrids. Scale bar 200 µm. Magnification 40X.
The absorbance intensity and fluorescence intensity of PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 in solutions with different pH value and PBS.
Hemolysis caused by different conjugates (1 µg).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Standard deviation is calculated from three samples. P<0.05.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of green emission polymer dots into pyropheophorbide-α enhance the PDT effect and biocompatibility

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Science and Technology, College of Chemistry and Biology, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Pharmaceutical Science and Technology, College of Chemistry and Biology, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pudong New Area People's Hospital, Shanghai 201200, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bPudong New Area People's Hospital, Shanghai 201200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green emission up-conversion carbon-based polymer dots (CPDs) owned excellent photophysical properties and good solubility. Most photosensitizers (PS) are hydrophobic which limits their application in biomedicine. Herein we synthesized and integrated green emitting CPDs into pyropheophorbide-α (PPa) to improve the overall properties of the PS.

The nano-agent was incorporated through amide condensation and electrostatic interaction. The structure, size and morphology of the prepared conjugates were determined by FTIR, TEM, DLS, TGA, 1HNMR, Uv-vis, and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The dark and light toxicity, as well as cellular uptake, was also monitored on the human esophageal cancer cell line (Eca-109).

Our results illustrate that the conjugation improved the PDT efficacy by increasing the ROS generation. The nano-hybrids showed pH sensitivity as well as good hemocompatibility as the hemolysis ratio was decreased when treated with nano-conjugates. PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 had the pH response and stronger ability to absorb light and produce fluorescence in an acidic environment (pH 4.0 and pH 5.0) The synthesized nano-hybrids doesnot affect the clotting time. An increase in the absorbance wavelengths was observed. The results of MTT assay showed that dark toxicity was reduced after conjugation.
This CPDs-based drug enhanced tumor-inhibition efficiency as well as low dark toxicity in vitro, showing significant application potential for PDT-based treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots and polymer dots own remarkable features such as biocompatibility, high fluorescence yield and excellent resistance against photo- bleaching [1–4]. Therefore, carbonaceous materials are widely used in different fields such as biomedicine, and theranostics [5–7]. Due to their multiluminiscent centers, rich surface states, and matrix effects carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [8], having crosslinking structures of non-conjugated structures/molecules, used in different applications [9] The fluorescence of CPDs has been extensively discussed previously [10–12]. The CPDs own the prominent optical properties of the CDs as well as inherit the characteristics of the polymer [13,14]. CPDs typically display light absorption in the UV–vis region [15,16]. Recently researchers have focused their attention on the optical properties of CDs such as, the light-induced enhancement of fluorescence, [17] the up-conversion fluorescence emission, [18,19] emission induced by aggregation, the amplified emission, [20] and radiations transient processes of carriers. [19,21,22].

In photodynamic therapy (PDT), targeted cells are killed through singlet oxygen generated by photosensitizers (PSs). Therefore, the development of new and effective photosensitizers has been a hot topic of PDT research. The doping of nanoparticle (e.g. carbon dots or polymer dots) in to the photosensitizers is an approach to overcome the drawbacks of the photosensitizers used in PDT [23,24]. The incorporation of nanoparticle into the photosensitizer have several advantages: i) they can overcome the problem/ issue of poor solubility ii) display the improved permeability and retention (EPR) effect; iii) can be functionalized with different target compounds; [25] iv) can be modified into multipurpose platforms [23,26].

Conjugated polymers have been utilized for a variety of applications such as cell imaging, in vivo imaging, [27–31] biosensors, [32–34] drug delivery, [35] and PDT [36]. Researchers have put a lot of effort to amplify the singlet oxygen (1O2) generation for PDT applications. The energy transfer mechanism opens up new possibilities for multichromophoric photosensitizers [36]. The tissue penetration could be improved by increasing the conjugation length [37]. Photosensitizer molecules can be physically encapsulated in conjugated polymer nanoparticles by hydrophobic interactions [38,39]. The integration of multichromophoric conjugated polymer base nanoparticles into the photosensitizers enhances the ROS generation by energy transfer mechanism. But there are some drawbacks associated with the encapsulation approach such as (i) the photosensitizers tends to aggregate when conjugated with nanoparticles (ii) dark toxicity could be increase when photosensitizer leaches out from nanoparticle. The conjugation of cationic polymer and porphyrins was studied as an alternative approach to increase the ROS generation [40–43]. Additionally, the covalent linkage between cationic polythiophene and porphyrins increased the ROS generation through energy transfer mechanism [41].

The hydrophobic polymers, as compared to the hydrophilic conjugated polymers usually form densely packed small and stable nanoparticles [44]. The energy transfer efficiency and photobleaching issue could be improved by the covalent linkage of porphyrins in the backbone of the polymer. In vitro studies revealed that the conjugated Pdots could destroy the cancer cells when activated with an appropriate light dose [44]. These studies inspired us to integrate the polymer dots into the pyropheophorbide-α(PPa), a chlorophyll derivative to improve the PDT induced treatment. The green emission up-conversion polymer dots were conjugated through covalent and non-covalent interactions and their photophysical and anti-tumor activity was studied and compared.

Pyropheophorbide-α (PPa) was purchased from Shanghai Xianhui Pharmaceuticles Co, Ltd. All other chemicals and reagents were acquired from Sinopharm chemical reagent co, Ltd. All reactions were protected from sunlight and all solutions were freshly prepared with ultra-pure water. 1H NMR spectra were recorded on a Bruker AMX-400 (United Kingdom) and chemical shifts δ (ppm) were referenced to TMS. UV-–vis absorption spectra were collected using an ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Model V-530, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FluoroMax-4, France). TEM images were taken from JEM-2100 (Japan) and FTIR of the CPD-hybrids was recorded on Perkin Elmer, spectrum-Two FT-IR (USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were done on a dynamic light scattering instrument BI-200SM (China). TGA analysis were measured using TG209F1 Libra (Germany).

The up-conversion photoluminescence CPDs were synthesized according to Huo et al. [45]. Briefly, 108 mg of catechol was dissolved in 15 mL deionized water and then 120 μL ethanediamine was added, followed by ultra-sonication for 10–20 min. The mixture was heated at 180 °C in (polytetrafluoroethylene) for 12 hrs. The mixture was then cooled and filtered, centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and freeze dried ( Fig. 1
, i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of PPa-CPDs through physical interaction (PPa-CPD1)
ce:section.ce:para:
The non-covalent interaction (π–π stacking) between CPDs and pyropheophorbide-α (PPa) was achieved following a method described by Matshitse et al. [46]. The CPDs (30 mg dissolved in 1 mL water) and pyropheophorbide-α (36.0 mg, 0.065 mmol) were dissolved in dry DMF (2.0 mL). The mixture was sonicated for 4 h and then stirred for 48 h, at ambient temperature, followed by successive washing with, deionized water and ethanol by centrifugation to remove the unreacted entities. The product was finally washed with diethyl ether and air dried ( Fig. 1
, ii).

In the synthesis of PPa-CPD conjugate (PPa-PD2), PPa (25 mg, 0.043 mol) and HBTU (15.0 mg, 1.2 M) were dissolved in 2.0 mL dry DMF, 2.5 M of DIPEA, and 30 mg (dissolved in 1 mL water) of CPDs was added and stirred at room temperature for 12–16 h. The product was then successively washed with deionized water, ethanol followed by centrifugation for 15 min. The dark color product was washed with ether and dried ( Fig. 1
, iii).

The structure of the synthesized carbon-based polymer dots (CPDs) and their conjugates was analyzed using TEM, FTIR TGA, DLS and 1HNMR. Absorption and emission spectra were observed in different solvents to confirm the solvatochromism of the prepared drugs. Absorbance spectra and emission spectra were collected from 300 nm to 800 nm in 1 nm steps. The as prepared conjugates were dissolved in different solvents i,e DMSO, DMF and, MeOH.

Eca-109 cells (5 × 104 cells/well) were cultured and grown in 12-well plates. After 24 h, the medium containing 2 μM compounds was added and then incubated for 24 h. The cellular uptake of the drugs by Eca-109 was determined according to the method previously reported using a fluorescence spectrophotometer [47].

The cellular uptake of the nano-hybrids was further confirmed by a fluorescence microscope. Eca-109 cells were cultured overnight and then 2 µM of each conjugate was added and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then observed under a microscope [48].

The synthesized conjugates were dissolved in PBS and then filtered with a 0.22 μm filter. Cells were cultured in 96 well plate at 5 × 104 cells /100 μL per well for 24 h, then treated with 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 µg/mL of PD-conjugates for dark toxicity and 0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1 µg/mL for phototoxicity. Laser light was irradiated with an intensity of 2 J/cm2, for 1 min 40 s. Subsequently, the MTT solution (20 μL) was added per well and incubated for 4 h. The absorbance was then measured with a microplate reader and the data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) [49]. The viability of the cells was calculated according to the following formula: cell viability (%) = OD experiment / OD control × 100%. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The cellular toxicity of the conjugates was further confirmed through a fluorescence microscope using live and dead staining (calcein).

The hemocompatibility of the conjugates was tested through hemolytic assays i.e. hemolysis ratio, and blood clotting test. The erythrocytes were collected by centrifuging the rabbit blood for 5 min at 16,000 rpm. The erythrocytes were then washed and diluted with PBS. Afterward, the diluted blood and conjugates solution (500 μL each) was added to the tubes. The distilled water (positive control) and PBS (negative control) were used as the control groups. The tubes were then incubated at 37 °C for 1 hour. After that, each tube was centrifuged for 5 min. The supernatant was collected and absorbance was measured at 545 nm with a microplate reader. The following formula was used to calculate the hemolysis ratio, [50];
Hemolysis
ratio
( HR )
=
[
(
Ap − Ab
)
/
(
At − Ab
)
]
× 100 %
Where, Ap refers to the absorbance of each conjugated drug group, At denotes the absorbance of the positive control and Ab indicates the absorbance of DPBS. The experiment was performed in triplicate.

The effect of the conjugated drugs on blood clotting was evaluated. A volume of 50 μL of recalcified whole-blood solution (0.2 M CaCl2, 10 mM in the blood) was added to the drug (37 °C) in polypropylene tubes. Then, the tube was incubated at 37 °C for 30 s, 60 s, 90 s, and 120 s, respectively. Blood was used as a control group. After pre-set time 1 mL of distilled water was added gently without disturbing the clot. The absorbance of the supernatant was recorded at 540 nm by using a microplate reader. Three replicates were performed.

The synthesized nano-hybrids were tested to evaluate the pH-sensitive response in different solutions (pH 4–8). Each CPD conjugate (10 μM) was dissolved in different pH solutions. Then the changes in emission and absorption peaks were recorded through a fluorescence spectrophotometer [51] and UV–vis spectrophotometer [45].

The photo-stability is an important characteristic of light-dependent drugs. The synthesized conjugates were dissolved in DMSO and their absorbance was recorded after irradiation at a different time interval (0–90 min). The wavelength of the semiconductor laser unit was 670 nm and the output power was 5 mW/cm2. The rate of photo-bleaching was calculated according to the equation: percent (%) = At / A0 ×100, where A0 represents the absorbance before irradiation and At represents the absorbance of the solution after irradiation while t is the irradiation time.

The 1,3-Diphenylisobenzofura (DPBF) and each CPD conjugate (2 µM) was mixed at a 30 to 1 ratio followed by irradiation with a Nd: YAG laser(670 nm) at an interval of every 5 s. The laser intensity was 5 mW/cm2. The decrease in peak at 417 imparts the consumption of DPBF was measured using Uv-vis spectrophotometer. The decrease in intensity refers to the amount of singlet oxygen generated by nano-hybrid. The rate of ROS generation was calculated by the following equation,

ln (
[ DPBF ] t / [ DPBF ] 0
) = · kt
Where [DPBF]t refers to the concentration of DPBF after laser treatment and [DPBF]0 refers to the concentrations of DPBF before irradiation. Values of k are the rate of singlet oxygen generation and t indicates the duration of irradiation [52].

Graphs were created by origin 8.0 (Graph Software, USA). All results are presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was determined by unpaired two-tailed t tests or two-way analysis of variance. A P value of <0.01 and <0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CPDs and PPa-CPD conjugates
ce:section.ce:para:
FTIR of the up-conversion CPDs and their conjugated hybrids is shown in Fig. 2
. The broad band observed around 3000–3600 cm−1 attributed to the presence of NH and OH. The peak around 2960 cm−1 represents ­CH2 while the band at 1351 cm−1 refers to the CC (CPDs). The shoulder around 1660 cm−1 extends in the conjugated drugs. The peak around 1500–1600 cm−1 refers to the C = C stretching. The comparison of FTIR of CPDs and their conjugates indicates the formation of new complexes. The 1HNMR of PPa and conjugates depicts the conjugation of the photosensitizer (Fig. S1a-c). The peak at −2 ppm suggests the presence of PPa in the conjugates. The appearance of a peak around 7.4–7.5 ppm refers to the amide bond formation. TEM images of the polymer dots and their conjugates are illustrated in Fig. 2
b-d. The morphology and size of the conjugates attribute to the successful conjugation. The size of the conjugates was studied using DLS measurement. The sizes of PPa-CPD1, and PPa-CPD2 were observed to be 35.2 nm and 25 nm respectively, compared to CPDs alone at 2.67 nm. The results of DLS measurement showed an increase in the sizes of the conjugates indicating the integration of PPa into the CPDs. Since porphyrins appear to accumulate as they interact with nanoparticles due to porphyrin adsorption into the nanoparticles, the increase in size after incorporation indicates aggregation. Aggregation by π-π stacking is popular in porphyrins [53].

The study of the optical properties of the drugs is crucial for their applications in biomedicine. The Uv-vis absorption spectra of the conjugates were shown in Fig. 3
a, b. The absorbance bands of the conjugated drugs showed strong peaks at 415 and 671 nm in MeOH. The PPa has two peaks a strong Soret band around 410–415 nm and a weak Q band around 667 nm. The absorbance of PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 in DMSO and DMF was 671 nm while the peaks appear at 669 nm in MeOH for PPa-CPD2. The fluorescence emission spectra of PPa-CPD1 showed shifted emission peaks from 640 nm (DMSO) to 674 nm and 676 nm in DMF and MeOH respectively. The fluorescence peaks (PPa-CPD2) at 670, 671 and 672 nm were observed in the solution of DMF, DMSO and MeOH. The shift in the peaks of the conjugate attributes to the successful conjugation and purity of conjugates. The green fluorescence signal was attributed to the various surface functional groups of the CPDs. Organic drugs are known to show different fluorescence in different solvents termed as a solvatochromic effect [54,55]. The CPDs functionalized PPa showed solvatochromism.

One of the important aspects of photosensitizers is their photostability. The previous studies showed that the hydrophilic groups of Pdots significantly affect the stability and internal structure of pdots and the doped molecule [56].To investigate their stability, the ODs of the conjugates dissolved in DMSO were measured. Fig. 5
a-b depicted that after irradiation for 90 min the as synthesized conjugates showed no significant decrease, thus solve the problem of photobleaching.

A good photosensitizer must be able to generate ROS efficiently, which is a key to PDT. The ROS generation of the conjugates was determined using DPBF as a quencher and PPa as a reference in DMF [57]. The DPBF intensity is known to decrease when it interacts with the ROS generated by the conjugates. After irradiation, the nano-conjugates generated ROS and the generation was higher as compared to the precursor drug ( Fig. 5
d-f). Our analysis showed that the ROS generation was increased after conjugation with Pdots. Without a doubt the conjugates could be used as potential tumor treating drugs.

The cellular absorption of the conjugates was tested using fluorescence spectrophotometry and fluorescence microscopy. Eca-109 cells were incubated with the conjugates for 24 h and the cells were observed under a fluorescence microscope ( Fig. 6
a, b). The uptake was tested again using a fluorescence spectrophotometer after different time intervals ( Fig. 6
c). The fluorescence microscope images together with the spectrophotometry results confirmed that the conjugates were absorbed by the cells.

Thermogravimograms of the CPDs and their conjugates conducted in the temperature range of 50–800 °C in the air were illustrated in Fig. 6
d. In comparison with the CPDs (92.8%) the TGA curve of PPa-CPD1 revealed a weight loss of 96% and 88% (PPa-CPD2) respectively. It was reported that the small molecules have low melting points thus result in low thermal stability. The weight loss of CPDs was less than PPa-CPD1 which might be due to the polymerization of CPDs. Different functional groups present on the surface of carbonized molecules results in different oxidative behaviors [46]. The weight loss of the conjugates could indicate thermal stability associated with the interplay between size and degree of functionalization of the nanoparticles.

Further investigations were executed to assess the in vitro therapeutic effect of the conjugates in Eca-109 cells. As shown in Fig. 7
b and Table S1, it could be seen that the dark cytotoxicity of PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 was significantly lower than that of PPa at examined concentrations (0–5 µg/mL). The TC50 (half inhibitory concentration without irradiation) of PPa-CPD1, PPa-CPD2, and PPa calculated by SPSS 19.0 software was 3.108 µg/mL, 4.422 µg/mL, and 0.145 µg/mL, respectively. The light induced cytotoxicity was investigated when the concentrations of the samples were from 0 µg/mL to 1 µg/mL. After irradiation with a light intensity of 2 J/cm2 for 1 min 40 s, the cell viability of PPa-CPD1, PPa-CPD2, and PPa all decreased in a drug concentration- dependent manner. The IC50 (half inhibitory concentration under light) values were found to be 0.068 µg/mL, 0.066 µg/mL, and 0.275 µg/mL corresponding to PPa, PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2, respectively. The “therapeutic ratio” values of PPa, PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 obtained by comparing TC50 and IC50 values were 2.13, 47.1, and 16.08, respectively. The result suggested that integrating CPDs into PPa could significantly reduce the dark toxicity of PPa and didn't affect the photodynamic anti-tumor ability of PPa, and PPa-CPD1 could be more effective and less toxic than PPa-CPD2 and PPa used as a photosensitizer.

Live and dead staining is a simple and rapid method to evaluate the efficacy of the conjugate as potential photosensitizers. Thus the dark and light-dependent toxicity was further validated through a fluorescence microscope. The green fluorescence (calcein) corresponded to the number of viable/live cells and Fig. 7
a illustrated that the number of live cells was reduced when treated with PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 in the presence of light. The cell staining results and MTT experiment results were consistent, and indicated that the conjugates could be used as a potent photosensitizer for PDT.

The hemocompatibility of the as-synthesized conjugates was tested through hemolysis ratio and clotting time. The hemocompatibility evaluation is a prerequisite for any drug to be used for treatment (Fig. S2a). The color of the supernatant obtained depicts the hemolytic ratio of the erythrocytes when treated with 1 µg of conjugates and their precursors. Drug groups presented light yellow similar to PBS control group, while the positive group was bright red. The quantitative data of the hemolysis ratio was shown in table 2
. PPa-CPD1 and PPa-CPD2 showed a low hemolysis ratio of 1.18 and 0.98% revealing its best hemocompatibility which was lower than PPa (1.79%). If the hemolysis rate is below 5%, medical materials were considered as non-hemolysis according to national biological safety [58]. The results of the samples were all less than 5%, conforming to the national biological material hemolysis rate security specified requirements. The result of the hemolysis ratio suggested that incorporation of CPDs with the precursor drug improved hemocompatibility and maintained hemostasis as the incorporated drug showed no effect on blood clotting (Fig. S2b). The results showed that OD of the control was higher than the drug group during the first 30 s, but at the end of 120 s, the OD of hybrid drugs was the lower than the control, suggesting that the conjugation of CPDs to PS help maintain hemostasis of the body fluids.

Photodynamic therapy is a treatment modality which utilizes light to activate a light sensitive drug termed as photosensitizer which in turn reacts with molecular oxygen and generates ROS. The production of ROS is critical for this treatment approach. Hence, photosensitizers are important for photodynamic therapy. The photosensitizers which are currently available possess some drawbacks such as poor water solubility and cytotoxicity etc. So, very small amount could be used for treatment. During the last few years different approaches have been proposed to overcome the problems of photosensitizers. Conjugating nanoparticles to the photosensitizers is an approach which piqued the attraction of researchers. Nanoparticles such as carbons dots (CDs) and carbon based polymer dots (CPDs) owned good properties such as excellent solubility, low cytotoxicity and optical properties. In 2019 Jose et al. incorporated protoporphyrin IX into carbon-dots and showed that the conjugation improved the bio-imaging but ROS generation was reduced. [59] Previously, we conjugated red emission carbon based polymer dots (R-CPDs) and integrated them into PPa [60]. The studies on PDT effects showed that the conjugated nanohybrids possessed high ROS generation and good biocompatibility. In this study we synthesized and conjugated green emission photoluminiscent polymer dots with pyropheophorbide-α (PPa), to improve the overall PDT effects of the photosensitizers. The PDT effect of the conjugated nano-drugs was enhanced when compared to the precursor photosensitizer. The dark toxicity of the conjugated hybrids was reduced and the hybrids also possessed the pH sensitivity of the CPDs enabling them to be used as sensor in tumor acidic environment. The covalent interaction between the two components increased the photo-stability. The results of hemolysis ratio and blood clotting test revealed that the synthesized nano-hybrids maintained the normal physiological environment. The high intensity at pH 4 and 5 attributes that the conjugates could be used as a sensor for imaging tumor cells. The conjugation of R-CPDs and CPDs both increased the ROS generation but nano-drugs with CPDs showed a wide range of pH sensitivity because the green emission CPDs are functionalized with different groups. In short, the covalent and π-π interaction between PPa and CPDs improved the PDT efficacy of photosensitizer. Moreover, the cytotoxicity was reduced without light treatment enabling them a potential photosensitizer in the field of bioimaging.

The up-conversion carbon-based polymer dots (CPDs) are extensively studied and used in biomedicine due to their enticing properties such as low dark toxicity, and good optical properties. In this paper, we synthesized green fluorescence up-conversion pdots and conjugated them with pyropheohorbide-α through π-π stacking and covalent linkage. Pyropheophorbide-α is a known photosensitizer with high ROS generation but possess dark toxicity and solubility problems. Because of these drawbacks very small quantity could be used for PDT. To overcome the problems of the PSs we incorporated PPa into CPDs. The as synthesized supra-molecule hybrids were characterized through optical studies, FTIR, TEM, DLS, and 1HNMR. The absorbance of the conjugates was increased while a decrease in fluorescence was observed. The decrease in fluorescence was might be due to the aggregation of the nano-hybrids which is more common in π-π stacking. TGA analysis also confirms the conjugation between two molecules. The cellular uptake experiments determined the absorbance of the hybrids by the cells.

The cellular toxicity in the absence and presence of laser was monitored through MTT assay and fluorescence microscopy. Our results showed The ROS generation of the hybrids was increased so was the PDT effect. Whilst the dark toxicity was reduced significantly after conjugation. The nano-hybrids possessed high stability as well as showed pH sensitivity in different solutions inherited from CPDs. Therefore, CPD-conjugates could be used as nano-sensor. The effect of the drugs on hemostasis was tested through the determination of hemolysis ratio and blood clotting which proved that the drugs are safe to be used in biomedicines. The low dark toxicity, good photostability and good PDT efficacy make them potential photosensitizer for biomedical applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21977016), Foundation of Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (No. 18411968000, 18410721700, 18430731600, 19410711000, 19411970600, 20430730900, 20490740400), and Foundation of Science and Technology Commission of Pudong New Area (PKJ2019-Y31, PKJ2020-Y42).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.pdpdt.2021.102562.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolor fluorescent semiconducting polymer dots with narrow emissions and high brightness
ACS Nano

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Rong, C.F. Wu, J.B. Yu, X.J. Zhang, F.M. Ye, M. Zeigler, et al. Multicolor fluorescent semiconducting polymer dots with narrow emissions and high brightness. ACS Nano., 7 (2013), pp. 376–384.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, C. Sun, X.R. Chen, Y. Zhang, V.L. Colvin, Q. Rice, et al. Excitation wavelength independent visible color emission of carbon dots. Nanoscale., 9 (2017), pp. 1909–1915.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 53% efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white-light emitting diodes
